item.title: BusyTasks [System Monitoring]
item.itunes:title: BusyTasks [System Monitoring]
item.itunes:author: unknown-spirit
item.itunes:summary:
BusyTasks
(System Monitoring)
[b]Side (and sad) note:[/b]
In contrast to Unix, Windows is very obsessed with marking my program as malware. Of course, it is not. You may have to add BusyTasks to the list of exceptions in Microsoft Defender Antivirus before starting it.

This program uses several command line tools to receive its input. Please read the manual (see files) before installation!
Moreover, I'd be very grateful for any kind of feedback.

Isn't everything some kind of process? At least on a computer it is. So let's keep an eye on them. This program is a revival of my [URL=https://www.gnome-look.org/p/1002049/]BusyTasks plasmoid for the KDE4 desktop[/URL]. Due to several issues with KDE5 I decided to switch to java and give it one more chance to live and prosper there. It uses several programs to get its input. The collected data is neither unique nor new, but the idea to clearly sum it up in one single program is. This task monitor displays up to four different tasks, each with different priority.
BusyTasks is only useful if it is always visible (e.g. residing on a panel) If you had got to minimize windows or show the desktop to have a glance on it you could equally start the system monitor.
I recommend splitting up the taskbar, putting it in between and setting it up as a scheduled task.

The Linux version for java is outdated, I recommend using my KDE Plasmoid called [url=https://www.pling.com/find?search=BusyTasks]BusyTasksPlasmoid[/url].
For the moment this program is only released for Windows.
[read more]
item.description:
BusyTasks
(System Monitoring)
[b]Side (and sad) note:[/b]
In contrast to Unix, Windows is very obsessed with marking my program as malware. Of course, it is not. You may have to add BusyTasks to the list of exceptions in Microsoft Defender Antivirus before starting it.

This program uses several command line tools to receive its input. Please read the manual (see files) before installation!
Moreover, I'd be very grateful for any kind of feedback.

Isn't everything some kind of process? At least on a computer it is. So let's keep an eye on them. This program is a revival of my [URL=https://www.gnome-look.org/p/1002049/]BusyTasks plasmoid for the KDE4 desktop[/URL]. Due to several issues with KDE5 I decided to switch to java and give it one more chance to live and prosper there. It uses several programs to get its input. The collected data is neither unique nor new, but the idea to clearly sum it up in one single program is. This task monitor displays up to four different tasks, each with different priority.
BusyTasks is only useful if it is always visible (e.g. residing on a panel) If you had got to minimize windows or show the desktop to have a glance on it you could equally start the system monitor.
I recommend splitting up the taskbar, putting it in between and setting it up as a scheduled task.

The Linux version for java is outdated, I recommend using my KDE Plasmoid called [url=https://www.pling.com/find?search=BusyTasks]BusyTasksPlasmoid[/url].
For the moment this program is only released for Windows.
[read more]
item.content:encoded:
BusyTasks
(System Monitoring)
[b]Side (and sad) note:[/b]
In contrast to Unix, Windows is very obsessed with marking my program as malware. Of course, it is not. You may have to add BusyTasks to the list of exceptions in Microsoft Defender Antivirus before starting it.

This program uses several command line tools to receive its input. Please read the manual (see files) before installation!
Moreover, I'd be very grateful for any kind of feedback.

Isn't everything some kind of process? At least on a computer it is. So let's keep an eye on them. This program is a revival of my [URL=https://www.gnome-look.org/p/1002049/]BusyTasks plasmoid for the KDE4 desktop[/URL]. Due to several issues with KDE5 I decided to switch to java and give it one more chance to live and prosper there. It uses several programs to get its input. The collected data is neither unique nor new, but the idea to clearly sum it up in one single program is. This task monitor displays up to four different tasks, each with different priority.
BusyTasks is only useful if it is always visible (e.g. residing on a panel) If you had got to minimize windows or show the desktop to have a glance on it you could equally start the system monitor.
I recommend splitting up the taskbar, putting it in between and setting it up as a scheduled task.

The Linux version for java is outdated, I recommend using my KDE Plasmoid called [url=https://www.pling.com/find?search=BusyTasks]BusyTasksPlasmoid[/url].
For the moment this program is only released for Windows.
[read more]

item.title: Something for Poco F3/Redmi k40/Mi 11x [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:title: Something for Poco F3/Redmi k40/Mi 11x [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:author: xeesan
item.itunes:summary:
Something for Poco F3/Redmi k40/Mi 11x
(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))
a fork of AOSPA, with extra features and a brand new design
Like a asteroid or a minor planet orbiting around AOSPA
SomethingOS do not pretend to be better than any other, take that fits you the best and if you decide to install Something, thank you !
[read more]
item.description:
Something for Poco F3/Redmi k40/Mi 11x
(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))
a fork of AOSPA, with extra features and a brand new design
Like a asteroid or a minor planet orbiting around AOSPA
SomethingOS do not pretend to be better than any other, take that fits you the best and if you decide to install Something, thank you !
[read more]

Something for Poco F3/Redmi k40/Mi 11x

Like a asteroid or a minor planet orbiting around AOSPA
SomethingOS do not pretend to be better than any other, take that fits you the best and if you decide to install Something, thank you !

item.title: BOOTLOOP SAVER [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:title: BOOTLOOP SAVER [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:author: revwhiteshadow
item.itunes:summary:
BOOTLOOP SAVER
(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))
BOOTLOOP SAVER by revWhiteShadow is a revolutionary Magisk module designed to automatically disable any problematic modules causing bootloop issues on your Android device. Developed by the talented developer revWhiteShadow, this module acts as a safeguard, ensuring your device boots smoothly without being hindered by conflicting or problematic modules. It's the go-to solution for Magisk users who want a hassle-free, error-free booting experience. With BOOTLOOP SAVER, you can enjoy the benefits of Magisk modules while maintaining the stability and reliability of your Android device.
[h2]Virtualisation Installation[/h2]
Step 1: Open LDPlayer,android studio or other simulation
Ensure LDPlayer, an Android emulator, is installed on your computer. Open LDPlayer to begin the process.

Step 2: Follow Android Installation
Within LDPlayer, follow the steps outlined in the Android Installation section:

Open Magisk or KernelSU within the emulator.
Navigate to the Modules section.
Select the module from storage.
Install the module.
Reboot the virtual device within LDPlayer.
[read more]
item.description:

(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))

It's the go-to solution for Magisk users who want a hassle-free, error-free booting experience. [read more]

BOOTLOOP SAVER
(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))
BOOTLOOP SAVER by revWhiteShadow is a revolutionary Magisk module designed to automatically disable any problematic modules causing bootloop issues on your Android device. Developed by the talented developer revWhiteShadow, this module acts as a safeguard, ensuring your device boots smoothly without being hindered by conflicting or problematic modules. It's the go-to solution for Magisk users who want a hassle-free, error-free booting experience. With BOOTLOOP SAVER, you can enjoy the benefits of Magisk modules while maintaining the stability and reliability of your Android device.
[h2]Virtualisation Installation[/h2]
Step 1: Open LDPlayer,android studio or other simulation
Ensure LDPlayer, an Android emulator, is installed on your computer. Open LDPlayer to begin the process.

Step 2: Follow Android Installation
Within LDPlayer, follow the steps outlined in the Android Installation section:

Open Magisk or KernelSU within the emulator.
Navigate to the Modules section.
Select the module from storage.
Install the module.
Reboot the virtual device within LDPlayer.
[read more]

item.title: GS GRAPHIC RENDERINGS [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:title: GS GRAPHIC RENDERINGS [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:author: revwhiteshadow
item.itunes:summary:
GS GRAPHIC RENDERINGS
(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))
GS Graphic Renderings Tweak is a powerful tool designed to enhance the graphic rendering capabilities of Android devices. With this tweak, users have the freedom to choose between three advanced rendering options: OpenGL ES, Sikagl, and VulkanSika.
OpenGL ES, a widely-used graphics API, provides a solid foundation for rendering graphics on Android devices. It offers a high level of compatibility and performance, ensuring smooth and visually appealing graphics for gaming and multimedia applications.

Sikagl, an innovative rendering framework, introduces cutting-edge features and optimizations to deliver stunning visuals. With Sikagl, users can experience enhanced shading, lighting, and special effects, pushing the boundaries of graphic rendering on their Android devices.

VulkanSika, a modern and efficient graphics API, takes advantage of the latest hardware capabilities to deliver unparalleled performance and visual fidelity. By leveraging multi-threading and low-level hardware control, VulkanSika optimizes resource utilization and provides a smooth and responsive user experience, especially for graphically-intensive applications.

With the GS Graphic Renderings Tweak, users can seamlessly switch between these rendering options based on their preferences and specific application requirements. Whether they seek compatibility, advanced features, or maximum performance, this tweak empowers users to unlock the full potential of their Android devices and enjoy a customized and immersive graphical experience.

[h2]Linux Installation[/h2]

Unzip the ZIP File:
unzip
Navigate to the Extracted Folder:

cd extracted_folder
Run the Installer:

For 32-bit systems:
./
For 64-bit systems:
./

[h2]Windows Installation[/h2]
Extract the zip:
Use a tool like WinRAR, 7-Zip, or the built-in Windows tool to extract the contents of the APK file.
Open Command Prompt:

Press Win + R, type cmd, and press Enter.
Run the Installer:

For 32-bit systems:
bash
For 64-bit systems:
bash

[h2]Android Installation / BlissOS / Other Emulator Installation[/h2]
Open Magisk / Kernel SU:

Ensure you have Magisk or Kernel SU installed on your device or emulator.
Go to the Module Section:

Open the Magisk app and navigate to the Modules section.
Select the Module from Storage:

Tap on "Select from Storage" and choose the previously downloaded file.
Install the Module:

After selecting the file, press "OK". The module will install itself.
Reboot the Device:

Once the installation is complete, reboot your device to finalize the installation.

[h2]Virtualisation Installation[/h2]
Step 1: Open LDPlayer,android studio or other simulation
Ensure LDPlayer, an Android emulator, is installed on your computer. Open LDPlayer to begin the process.

Step 2: Follow Android Installation
Within LDPlayer, follow the steps outlined in the Android Installation section:

Open Magisk or KernelSU within the emulator.
Navigate to the Modules section.
Select the module from storage.
Install the module.
Reboot the virtual device within LDPlayer.
[read more]

VulkanSika, a modern and efficient graphics API, takes advantage of the latest hardware capabilities to deliver unparalleled performance and visual fidelity. VulkanSika, a modern and efficient graphics API, takes advantage of the latest hardware capabilities to deliver unparalleled performance and visual fidelity. By leveraging multi-threading and low-level hardware control, VulkanSika optimizes resource utilization and provides a smooth and responsive user experience, especially for graphically-intensive applications.

With the GS Graphic Renderings Tweak, users can seamlessly switch between these rendering options based on their preferences and specific application requirements. After selecting the file, press "OK". The module will install itself.
Reboot the Device:

Open the Magisk app and navigate to the Modules section.
Select the Module from Storage:

Tap on "Select from Storage" and choose the previously downloaded file.
Install the Module:

After selecting the file, press "OK". The module will install itself.
Reboot the Device:

Once the installation is complete, reboot your device to finalize the installation.

[h2]Virtualisation Installation[/h2]
Step 1: Open LDPlayer,android studio or other simulation
Ensure LDPlayer, an Android emulator, is installed on your computer. Open LDPlayer to begin the process.

Step 2: Follow Android Installation
Within LDPlayer, follow the steps outlined in the Android Installation section:

Open Magisk or KernelSU within the emulator.
Navigate to the Modules section.
Select the module from storage.
Install the module.
Reboot the virtual device within LDPlayer.
[read more]
item.content:encoded:
PHOENIX SDM 7
(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))
[url=https://www.pling.com/p/2050006/]MTK Optimization [/url]
[url=https://www.pling.com/p/2050005/]SDM 8 Optimization [/url]
[url=https://www.pling.com/p/2050004/]SDM 7 Optimization [/url]
[url=https://www.pling.com/p/2019745/]SDM 6 Optimization [/url]

[h2]Linux Installation[/h2]

Unzip the ZIP File:
unzip
Navigate to the Extracted Folder:

cd extracted_folder
Run the Installer:

For 32-bit systems:
./
For 64-bit systems:
./

[h2]Windows Installation[/h2]
Extract the zip:
Use a tool like WinRAR, 7-Zip, or the built-in Windows tool to extract the contents of the APK file.
Open Command Prompt:

Press Win + R, type cmd, and press Enter.
Run the Installer:

For 32-bit systems:
bash
For 64-bit systems:
bash

[h2]Android Installation / BlissOS / Other Emulator Installation[/h2]
Open Magisk / Kernel SU:

Ensure you have Magisk or Kernel SU installed on your device or emulator.
Go to the Module Section:

Open the Magisk app and navigate to the Modules section.
Select the Module from Storage:

Tap on "Select from Storage" and choose the previously downloaded file.
Install the Module:

After selecting the file, press "OK". The module will install itself.
Reboot the Device:

Once the installation is complete, reboot your device to finalize the installation.

[h2]Virtualisation Installation[/h2]
Step 1: Open LDPlayer,android studio or other simulation
Ensure LDPlayer, an Android emulator, is installed on your computer. Open LDPlayer to begin the process.

Step 2: Follow Android Installation
Within LDPlayer, follow the steps outlined in the Android Installation section:

Open Magisk or KernelSU within the emulator.
Navigate to the Modules section.
Select the module from storage.
Install the module.
Reboot the virtual device within LDPlayer.
[read more]

item.title: GODSPEED MODE A.I. [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:title: GODSPEED MODE A.I. [System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS)]
item.itunes:author: revwhiteshadow
item.itunes:summary:
GODSPEED MODE A.I.
(System Tools (NOT OS or ROMS))
This Magisk Module will start automatic with boot,there is 2 profiles in this module
[h2]Linux Installation[/h2]

Unzip the ZIP File:
unzip
Navigate to the Extracted Folder:

cd extracted_folder
Run the Installer:

For 32-bit systems:
./
For 64-bit systems:
./

[h2]Windows Installation[/h2]
Extract the zip:
Use a tool like WinRAR, 7-Zip, or the built-in Windows tool to extract the contents of the APK file.
Open Command Prompt:

Press Win + R, type cmd, and press Enter.
Run the Installer:

For 32-bit systems:
bash
For 64-bit systems:
bash

[h2]Android Installation / BlissOS / Other Emulator Installation[/h2]
Open Magisk / Kernel SU:

Ensure you have Magisk or Kernel SU installed on your device or emulator.
Go to the Module Section:

Open the Magisk app and navigate to the Modules section.
Select the Module from Storage:

Tap on "Select from Storage" and choose the previously downloaded file.
Install the Module:

After selecting the file, press "OK". The module will install itself.
Reboot the Device:

Once the installation is complete, reboot your device to finalize the installation.
